title: Listening - Part 4: Changing Career to Craftsmanship
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people who left office jobs to pursue traditional crafts

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d spent fifteen years as a data analyst, staring at spreadsheets until my eyes blurred. [21]I reached a point where I couldn't bear the thought of another day producing nothing more than a digital report[/21]. I needed to create something I could actually hold. I took up bespoke furniture making, and though the financial transition was terrifying, [26]the sheer pride I feel when I run my hand over a finished oak table is incomparable to any corporate achievement[/26]. It’s a slow, methodical process that requires absolute focus. My hands are constantly covered in sawdust and I’ve got more scars than I can count, but I’ve never felt more connected to my work. || Speaker 2: My career in public relations was fast-paced and glamorous, but ultimately, it felt hollow. [22]I was constantly managing crises that didn't really matter in the grand scheme of things[/22], and it took a toll on my mental health. I decided to retrain as a ceramicist. Working with clay is incredibly grounding. However, the business side of being a solo maker is a steep learning curve. [27]The most stressful aspect is the unpredictable nature of my monthly income[/27], which fluctuates wildly depending on craft fairs and online sales. One month I’m doing great, the next I’m wondering how to pay the studio rent. It’s a trade-off for my peace of mind. || Speaker 3: I was a software engineer, but [23]I’ve always been obsessed with the history of watchmaking and the intricate mechanics involved[/23]. I eventually quit to apprentice with a master horologist. It’s incredibly delicate work, and your eyes really feel the strain. People think I live a quiet, peaceful life now, but [28]the hardest part is the extreme isolation of spending ten hours a day alone at a workbench[/28]. I go days without having a proper conversation with anyone. I love the technical challenge, but I do miss the buzz of a busy office and the casual banter with colleagues during lunch breaks. || Speaker 4: I used to work in high-end fashion marketing, but [24]I became increasingly disillusioned by the ethical issues surrounding mass production and waste[/24]. I wanted to be part of the solution, so I started my own workshop using traditional weaving techniques and natural dyes. It’s a labor of love, truly. [29]What I find most rewarding is the deep appreciation my customers have for the heritage and time behind every piece[/29]. They aren't just buying a scarf; they’re buying into a story and a sustainable philosophy. That interaction makes the long, physically demanding hours at the loom feel like a privilege rather than a chore. || Speaker 5: After a decade in corporate law, I was completely burnt out. [25]I took a sabbatical that turned into a permanent career change after I discovered a hidden talent for blacksmithing[/25]. There’s something incredibly primal about working with fire and iron. It’s physically exhausting, but in a good way. The transition wasn't just about the work, though. [30]I’ve found that the most unexpected benefit has been the massive improvement in my physical fitness and stamina[/30]. I’m stronger at forty than I was at twenty-five. I used to have constant back pain from sitting at a desk, but swinging a hammer all day has completely cured that. I feel alive.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what prompted each speaker to change their career.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions he couldn't bear the thought of only producing digital reports and wanted a tangible product.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 felt her previous work was 'hollow' and she was managing 'crises that didn't really matter'.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 mentions being obsessed with the history and intricate mechanics of watchmaking.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 was disillusioned by ethical issues and waste in the fashion industry.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 discovered a hidden talent during a sabbatical and decided to change careers permanently.

options.C: a feeling that their work lacked significance
options.D: a need to earn a higher salary
options.E: a frustration with the intangible nature of their tasks
options.F: an unexpected discovery of a personal skill
options.G: concerns about the morality of an industry
options.H: a long-term fascination with a specific process

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as a significant outcome of their move.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 describes the 'sheer pride' of seeing a finished physical product.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 emphasizes the stress of an 'unpredictable' and 'fluctuating' monthly income.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 finds the 'extreme isolation' of working alone to be the hardest part.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 finds reward in the 'deep appreciation' and interaction with her customers.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 notes the 'massive improvement' in his physical fitness and strength.

options.C: the difficulty of finding a mentor
options.D: a sense of fulfillment in their craftsmanship
options.F: a positive impact on their physical health
options.H: the recognition of their work by experts
